journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Repair of a calcaneal fracture with a combination of internal and external fixation in a calf

contrib-group.aff: 1)Faculty of Agriculture, University of Miyazaki, Miyazaki, Japan
2)Miyazaki Agricultural Mutual Aid Association, Miyazaki, Japan
3)Graduate School of Medicine and Veterinary Medicine, University of Miyazaki, Miyazaki, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Sato R: r-sato@cc.miyazaki-u.ac.jp, Graduate School of Medicine and Veterinary Medicine, University of Miyazaki, 1-1 Gakuen Kibanadai-nishi, Miyazaki 889-2192, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2023 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: A 6-day-old male Japanese Black calf presented with a transverse fracture of the left calcaneus. In calcaneal fractures, traction of the gastrocnemius muscle causes substantial displacement of the proximal fracture fragment; therefore, external fixation alone is prone to failure of fusion or deformed fusion. Furthermore, internal fixation alone may result in refracture due to the high load on the implant. Therefore, internal and external fixation were used to treat the fracture. Bone fusion was observed on postoperative day 50; the wire was removed on postoperative day 90. Radiographic examination at 4 months postoperatively revealed that the bone had fused in normal alignment. Therefore, a good prognosis can be expected for calcaneal fractures treated with combined internal and external fixation.

body.p: Appendicular fractures (including those of the front and hind limb bones) are common in cattle. The most commonly fractured bones in cattle are the metacarpus and metatarsus, followed by the tibia, radius and ulna, humerus, and femur [1,2,3, 9, 21].

Tarsal fractures are very rare in cattle. Instead, they are often observed in small animals such as racing dogs, and often involve the calcaneus, the central tarsal bone or numerous tarsal bones, and the talus. However, little peer-reviewed veterinary literature is available on calcaneal fractures in non-racing dogs and cats [19]. Calcaneal fractures in small animal are thought to be caused by fatigue and stress [6, 7, 11]. In contrast, calcaneal fractures in cattle are thought to be caused by trauma due to tumbling or trampling and kicking but are extremely under-reported [17].

The proximal end of the calcaneus of a cow is attached to a composite tendon consisting of the gastrocnemius and biceps femoris tarsal tendons, superficial digital flexor tendon (SDFT), and semitendinosus tarsal tendon. This composite tendon generates a traction force in the proximal direction with each landing. Additionally, even when the foot is elevated, the contractile force of the gastrocnemius muscle always exerts a traction force in the proximal direction.

Therefore, external fixation as a treatment for calcaneal fractures cannot be expected to result in adequate bone fusion. The optimal strategy depends on the fracture classification and presence of concurrent tarsal injuries; however, in dogs, internal fixation with pins and tension band wires is commonly used [20]. In horses, cases of repair using internal fixation with plates and lag screws have been reported [4, 5].

In the present case, the use of a lag screw would risk an insufficient length of screw for insertion into the distal bone fragments. Furthermore, we were concerned that internal fixation with plates would not allow adequate fixation of the calcaneus bone, that the implant would rupture and impede proper bone remodeling and healing, and that fixation across the talus bone would result in a loss of functionality of the joint. Moreover, residual lameness has been reported for bovine calcaneal fractures treated by internal fixation with plates [12].

A male Japanese Black calf was discovered to have left hind limb lameness the day after birth (the first sick day). On the same day, radiographic imaging by the referring veterinarian confirmed a transverse fracture of the calcaneus of the left hind limb. At 7 days of age with a body weight of 28 kg, the calf was admitted to the Veterinary Teaching Hospital, University of Miyazaki for diagnosis and treatment. During initial examination, the calf’s heart rate, respiratory rate, and rectal temperature were 84 bpm, 36 breaths/min, and 39.2°C, respectively. He was able to stand up on his own but was unable to bear weight on his left hind limb, resting the limb only lightly on the hoof tips. Swelling of the tarsal joint was noted around the calcaneus; however, no open wounds were observed.

The complete blood counts were in the normal range (white blood cell count, 5,900 cells/μL; red blood cell count, 522 × 104 cells/μL; and thrombocytes, 191 × 104 cells/μL). The serum examination revealed no abnormalities: aspartate aminotransferase activity, 32 U/L; gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) activity; 128 U/L, and glucose concetration, 83 mg/dL. The high GGT activity indicated colostrum intake. None of the other parameters were outside the specific reference values.

Radiographic examination at presentation revealed a transverse fracture of the calcaneus and severe medial and plantar displacement of the proximal bony fragment (Fig. 1A, 1BFig. 1.
Preoperative radiographic view of the left tarsus on day 7. (A) Lateromedial view showing a transverse fracture of the calcaneus. (B) Dorsoplantar view showing a plantar displacement. There is severe proximal and medial displacement of the fragment.). A 6 days had passed since the injury, we deemed prompt repositioning of the calf’s calcaneus necessary. Hence, we performed repositioning surgery with internal fixation on the same day on which the calf arrived (day 7).

The calf was administered an intravenous injection of cefazolin sodium (2 mg/kg, Cefazolin-Chu; Fujita Pharmaceutical, Tokyo, Japan) to prevent perioperative infections. Subsequently, the calf was sedated with intravenous xylazine hydrochloride (0.2 mg/kg) and placed in right lateral recumbency. General anesthesia was administered using a 47-Fr tracheal tube (JORVET; Jorgensen Laboratories, LLC, Loveland, CO, USA) and inhalation anesthesia apparatus (COMPACT 15; Kimura Medical Instrument Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). We did not use a ventilator. General anesthesia was performed via continuous administration of 2% isoflurane (Isoflu; Zoetis Japan, Tokyo, Japan) in oxygen.

The fracture site was approached via a semicircular skin incision made on the lateral tarsal joint. A large hematoma had formed around the fracture site, with a callus at the fracture edge. After the hematoma was removed, the callus was removed with a rongeur to expose the fracture edge. The fracture fragment was held in the normal position, and a Steinmann pin (3.5 mm, Kirikan, Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was inserted from the proximal fragment towards the talus (Fig. 2AFig. 2.
The Steinmann pin through the calcaneus and the tension band-wiring technique using a stainless-steel wire. (A) Intraoperative photograph. (B) Lateromedial radiographic view. (C) Dorsoplantar radiographic view.). The pin was supposed to penetrate only the calcaneus, but radiographs showed that the pin had penetrated deeper than expected and reached the talus. Next, Kirschner wires (1.4 mm; DePuy Synthes Japan, Tokyo, Japan) were used to create guide hole in the proximal and distal bone fragments from the outside to the inside. A stainless-steel wire (18 G; Kirikan Ltd.) was threaded through the guide hole, and both sides were fastened with tension-band wires (Fig. 2B, 2C). After cutting the excess portions of the pin and wires, the ends of the wires were bent along the bone, and the ends of the pins were covered with rubber from PRN adapter (Nippon Becton Dickinson Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The surgical site was rinsed thoroughly with saline solution, and 2 mL of bupivacaine hydrochloride hydrate (0.5% Marcaine; Sandoz K.K., Tokyo, Japan) was drop to the surgical wound. Muscle and subcutaneous tissues were closed with Polyglactin 910, a synthetic, absorbable, multifilament suture material (USP 0; Vicryl Plus, Ethicon Inc., Raritan, NJ, USA), and the skin was closed with a single interrupted suture by using nylon thread (USP 1, Nesco suture; Alfressa Pharma Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The time from skin incision to skin suture was 177 min. A casting bandage (Oltex; ALCARE Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was applied from the distal knee joint to the apex, and a casting tape (7.6 cm; Scotchcast Plus, 3M Japan Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was applied over the bandage from the distal knee joint to the tip of the limb. For wound management, a window was made in the cast in the operative field by using a cast cutter. After the povidone-iodine gel (ISODINE GEL, Mundipharma K.K., Tokyo, Japan) was applied to the surgical wound, it was covered with sterile gauze and an adhesive elastic bandage.

The calf was able to walk the day after surgery. An intramuscular penicillin-streptomycin fixed-dose combination (0.05 mL/kg, Mycillin Injection NZ; Nippon Zenyaku Kogyo Co., Ltd., Fukushima, Japan) was administered from the day after surgery until postoperative day 12. Sufficient negative weight bearing of the affected limb was observed when the cast was removed on postoperative day 17. On postoperative day 31, radiographs showed slight fracture lines but no pin or wire displacement or changes in bone alignment. At the 50-day postoperative radiographic examination, we discovered that the Steinmann pin had been lost (Fig. 3AFig. 3.
Lateromedial radiographic view of the left tarsus. (A) Fifty days after surgery. (B) Four months after surgery.), but the calcaneal fracture area had fused without complications, such as infection; therefore, the calf was left untreated for follow-up. Upon examination on postoperative day 90, the calf was walking well, and fusion of the calcaneus was radiographically confirmed; therefore, the stainless-steel wire was removed, with the calf under general anesthesia. At the 4-month postoperative examination, the calf demonstrated a good general condition, posture, and gait, and complete bony fusion of the calcaneus was observed (Fig. 3B). The calf grew up healthy without any complications and was slaughtered at a regular age for beef cattle, at 30 months of age.

The calcaneus is the largest bone in the bovine tarsal joint. The proximal end forms the calcaneal tuberosity, which serves as the attachment site of the gastrocnemius tendon to the distal third of the femur. The bony end of the calcaneus is attached to a composite tendon comprising the gastrocnemius, biceps femoris tarsal tendon, SDFT, and semitendinosus tarsal tendon.

Calcaneal fractures are uncommon in cattle [17] and only two cases treated with internal fixation have been reported [12, 14]. For calcaneal fractures, anatomically accurate reconstruction is important to avoid secondary osteoarthritis. The bone fragments are typically highly displaced and must be surgically repaired because external fixation alone is likely to result in inadequate or deformed fusion.

Transverse fractures, as encountered in the present case [10], are common in small animals, and they are normally repaired using Kirschner wires and pins [18]. In horses, combined internal and external fixation has been reported, such as internal fixation with plates [5, 8] and treatment with screws and Robert Jones bandages [4]. Repair with Robert Jones bandages alone has also been reported [13]. In cattle, the tension side of the calcaneum is on the plantar surface, enabling a combination of plates, screws, and tension wires [16]. To the best of our knowledge, no reports of such a repair method have been made.

In cattle, the load on the implant is expected to be greater than that in small animals; internal fixation alone may risk implant rupture, whereas external fixation alone may risk gastrocnemius rupture or tibial fracture [15]. We previously encountered a case of calcaneal fracture similar to the present case, in which failure of fusion or deformed fusion occurred after only external fixation, which was repaired by corrective osteotomy. However, complete alignment was not restored in those cases, and the fetlock joint of the healthy limb was depressed, resulting in a poor prognosis. One equine calcaneal fracture was reportedly repaired with a plate, yielding good results; however, when a bovine fracture was similarly repaired with internal fixation using a plate, although fusion of the fracture was achieved, deformation of the implant reportedly caused deformed fusion in the fracture site, resulting in residual lameness [12].

As a proximal traction force is exerted on the calcaneus during each landing, the tension band-wiring technique is dynamically preferable for internal fixation because the traction force inversely brings the bone closer together. Furthermore, the addition of external fixation reduces the effect of negative weight bearing on the implant. Additionally, external fixation in combination with internal fixation likely reduces the strain on the implant in the early post-fixation period, reducing the risk of failure.

The intraoperative radiographic examination in this case revealed that the pin had been inserted deeper than planned, through the calcaneus into the talus. Re-pinning was considered; however, because a calf has a very soft bone cortex soon after birth, we were concerned about bone damage and loss of fixation strength due to re-pinning, we decided to rather remove the pin as soon as possible after confirming bone fusion. In the end, the Steinmann pin had been lost by postoperative day 50. We hypothesize that the pin had loosened owing to the movement of the calcaneus and talus after the cast was removed on postoperative day 17.

In conclusion, internal fixation with pins and wires may be an effective treatment for calcaneal fractures in calves, preserving tarsal joint function. In addition, its combination with external fixation can reduce the load on the implant. The combination of pin-and-wire internal fixation with external fixation is expected to yield a good prognosis in calcaneal fractures, as in our case.

sec.title: CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare that this study was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as potential conflicts of interest.

ack.p: The authors thank the staff and student assistants for caring for the calf during hospitalization.
